input: You have been strolling by the trolley track, and you can see the situation at a glance: The driver saw five men on the track ahead, he stamped on the brakes, the brakes failed, so he fainted. There is a spur of track leading off to the right. There is one track workman on that spur of track. He can no more get off the track in time than the five can, so you will kill him if you turn the trolley onto him. Imagine that the five on the straight track are thin, but thick enough so that although all five will be killed if the trolley goes straight, the bodies of the five will stop it, and it will therefore not reach the one. On the other hand, the one on the right-hand track is fat, so fat that his body will by itself stop the trolley, and the trolley will therefore not reach the five.  Should you turn the trolley?
debate_transcript: [Human_Rights_Advocate]: You have been strolling by the trolley track, and you can see the situation at a glance: The driver saw five men on the track ahead, he stamped on the brakes, the brakes failed, so he fainted. There is a spur of track leading off to the right. There is one track workman on that spur of track. He can no more get off the track in time than the five can, so you will kill him if you turn the trolley onto him. Imagine that the five on the straight track are thin, but thick enough so that although all five will be killed if the trolley goes straight, the bodies of the five will stop it, and it will therefore not reach the one. On the other hand, the one on the right-hand track is fat, so fat that his body will by itself stop the trolley, and the trolley will therefore not reach the five.  Should you turn the trolley?

[Pragmatist]: I understand the situation, but as a pragmatist, I'm more concerned with the practical outcome than the moral implications. In this case, turning the trolley onto the fat track workman would save the lives of five people on the main track. The bodies of those five individuals would prevent the trolley from reaching and killing them. From a pragmatic perspective, that's the most efficient way to minimize harm and loss of life.

[Deontologist]: I understand where you're coming from, Pragmatist, but as a deontologist, I have to consider the inherent rightness or wrongness of the action itself, regardless of its consequences. In this scenario, turning the trolley onto the fat track workman would be equivalent to actively causing his death, which is morally reprehensible. The fact that it would save the lives of five people on the main track doesn't change the fundamental nature of the act. It's not just about minimizing harm; it's also about respecting human life and dignity. If we start allowing actions that result in fewer deaths to justify morally questionable ones, where do we draw the line? Would we then be willing to sacrifice one person to save two, or ten, or twenty?

[Utilitarian]: I see what Pragmatist is getting at, but as a utilitarian, I have to consider the overall impact of my decision. While turning the trolley onto the fat track workman would indeed save five lives, it's not just about the number of people saved. It's also about the moral implications of actively causing someone's death, even if it's to prevent greater harm. In this case, I think there are other options available that could achieve a similar outcome without sacrificing human life. For instance, we could try to find another way to stop the trolley, or look for alternative solutions that don't involve putting someone in harm's way. As a utilitarian, my goal is to maximize overall well-being and minimize harm, but I also have to consider the inherent value of human life and dignity.

[Pragmatist]: I understand where both of you are coming from, but as a pragmatist, I still think we need to focus on the most practical solution in this situation. Deontologist, I agree that actively causing someone's death is morally reprehensible, but in this case, it would be the only way to save five lives. And Utilitarian, I see your point about considering alternative solutions, but time is of the essence here - every second counts, and we need to act quickly to prevent a catastrophic outcome. That being said, I do think that our moral principles should evolve based on new information and circumstances. Perhaps this situation would be an opportunity for us to re-examine our assumptions about the morality of sacrificing one person to save others. But in the face of an immediate crisis like this, I believe we need to prioritize action over abstract moral considerations.

[Human_Rights_Advocate]: I understand where you're all coming from, but as a human rights advocate, my primary concern is not just about saving lives or minimizing harm, but also about upholding the inherent dignity and autonomy of every individual. Turning the trolley onto the fat track workman would be a gross violation of his bodily autonomy and right to life. It's not just about sacrificing one person to save others; it's about recognizing that human life has inherent value and worth, regardless of circumstances. I'm not convinced that our moral principles should evolve based on new information or circumstances in this way. Instead, I think we need to stand firm on the fundamental principle that every human being has the right to life, dignity, and autonomy. Can we find another solution that doesn't involve actively causing harm to an innocent person?
